topic: Cursor Agent Best Practices 2026: Multi-File Edits, Parallel Agents & Rules

key_points: Plan before coding: senior devs plan first, giving agent concrete goals
Write specific prompts with file names, test patterns, and constraints
Review AI-generated code carefully — it can look right but be subtly wrong
Use git checkpoints before multi-file refactors
Run tests after every agentic change, not just at the end
Cursor 2.0 supports up to 8 simultaneous agents in isolated git worktrees
/multitask command runs async subagents to parallelize requests
Tasks must be independent — Agent B cannot depend on Agent A's output
Use 2-3 parallel agents to start, not the full 8, to avoid merge conflicts
Configure notifications so you know when parallel agents finish
Long-running agents can now run for 52+ hours autonomously
Planner-Worker-Judge architecture: planners create tasks, workers execute, judges evaluate
Define done-conditions upfront in an external file with testable criteria
Planning-first: agent proposes plan and waits for approval before execution
Periodic context resets combat drift and tunnel vision in long runs
.cursorrules is NOT loaded in Agent mode — must use .mdc format
Four activation modes: Always Apply, Auto Attached (globs), Agent Requested, Manual
Keep always-apply rules under 200 words; total under 2,000 tokens
Recommended structure: core.mdc, framework.mdc, architecture.mdc, testing.mdc, security.mdc
Migrate incrementally from .cursorrules to .mdc, one section at a time
Cursor automatically creates and manages git worktrees for each parallel agent
Each agent gets its own isolated workspace with separate files and changes
Typical workflow: main branch → worktrees for tests + docs → 2 parallel agents → PRs → merge
worktree.json configuration enables 'Build in Parallel' feature
Git discipline with feature branches prevents merge noise at scale

stats.fact: Companies merge 39% more PRs after Cursor's agent became the default
stats.source: University of Chicago study, analyzed across tens of thousands of Cursor users

stats.fact: 75% of developers say AI reduces their 'toil work' — tasks that hinder productivity or increase frustration
Cursor grew from $100M to $2B ARR in just 14 months
Cursor has 18% usage at work among developers, tied with Claude Code, behind GitHub Copilot at 29%
Cursor 2.0 supports up to 8 simultaneous parallel agents, each in isolated git worktrees
Long-running Cursor agents can now operate autonomously for 52+ hours
stats.source: Cursor long-running agents research preview announcement, 2026

stats.fact: Cursor v2.4 (January 2026) introduced subagents — independent agents that handle discrete subtasks in parallel, each with its own context window
Junior developers are more likely to accept code from Tab (autocomplete), while senior developers are more likely to accept code from agents
stats.source: University of Chicago Cursor usage analysis

angles: The .cursorrules silent failure: most developers don't know their rules are completely ignored in Agent mode — the .mdc migration is urgent
Parallel agents with git worktrees: a step-by-step playbook for real-world parallelization without merge chaos
The Planner-Worker-Judge architecture: how teams running 52-hour autonomous coding sessions structure their agents
AGENTS.md vs .cursor/rules: which context file does what and when to use each
Token efficiency for rules: why always-apply rules over 200 words are silently hurting performance
The specific prompt formula that improves agent success rates: file names + patterns + constraints
When NOT to use parallel agents: dependency chains and the hidden cost of 8-agent parallelism
